data.title: As the RED test writer, I can exit with 'no new tests needed' when existing coverage is sufficient or the task is GREEN-classified
data.description:
## Description

**What**: Update the RED test writer agent prompt to include an explicit exit path for tasks where no new tests are needed — either because the task is GREEN-classified or because existing test coverage is sufficient.
**Why**: Currently the RED test writer has no clean way to signal that a task does not need new tests. It must either write a test (even when unnecessary) or use the rejection format (which implies a problem).
**Scope**:
- IN: Add a new output format for "no new tests needed" (distinct from rejection)
- IN: RED test writer consumes the shared behavioral testing standard (rule 1: check existing coverage first)
- IN: Exit path when task is GREEN-classified and existing tests pass
- IN: Escape hatch when agent determines existing coverage is sufficient regardless of classification
- OUT: Modifying sprint orchestrator routing (separate story)
- OUT: Modifying the shared testing standard itself (separate story)

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, the RED test writer prompt includes an explicit "no new tests needed" exit path for GREEN-classified tasks and as an escape hatch when existing coverage is sufficient
  ← Satisfies: "The RED test writer prompt includes an explicit no new tests needed exit path for GREEN-classified tasks and as an escape hatch when the agent determines existing coverage is sufficient"
- When this story is complete, the RED test writer consumes the shared behavioral testing standard
  ← Satisfies: "All test-writing agent prompts consume a shared behavioral testing standard"
- When this story is complete, unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic
  ← Satisfies: TDD done-of-done

## Considerations

- [Testing] "No tests needed" exit path must not create a loophole — eval must cover both "exit early" and "write test" paths

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
